coredata.dc:title: Electrostatic adsorbing graphene quantum dot into nickel–based layered double hydroxides: Electron absorption/donor effects enhanced oxygen electrocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiaolong
Zheng, Xingqun
Hu, Xiaolin
Zhao, Qiannan
Li, Li
Yu, Peng
Jing, Chuan
Zhang, Yuxin
Huang, Guangsheng
Jiang, Bin
Xu, Chaohe
Pan, Fusheng
coredata.dc:description: Exploring electrocatalysts with optimal electronic structure and understanding the relationship between electronic structure and activity are highly required for designing advanced OER catalysts. Recently, introducing nanocarbon materials into LDHs has been demonstrated as an effective way to improve the OER activity; however, deeply understanding the regulating effect of nanocarbon on the electronic structure of LDHs is still a critical challenge. Herein, it is reported that the regulating of the surface electronic structure of nickel-based layered double hydroxides (NiM-LDHs (M = Fe, Co, Mn)) by adsorbing graphene quantum dot (GQDs). The Ni-based LDHs/GQDs delivers enhanced OER activity, and particularly NiFe LDH/GQDs achieves an ultralow overpotential of 189 mV (10 mA cm−2) and superior performance in rechargeable Zn–air battery tests. Combining with theoretical calculations and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, we ascribe the excellent OER activity of Ni-based LDHs/GQDs to the strong interaction between NiFe LDH and GQDs, which changes the charge distribution around metal ions and triggers the charge accumulation of the active Ni species. The above results demonstrate the significance of controlling the surface electronic structure for designing advanced 3d metal electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Grapheme quantum dots
Layered double hydroxides
Electronic structure
Electrocatalysts
Zn-air batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculate model of (a) NiFe LDH and (b) NiFe LDH/GQDs. (c) OER pathway for NiFe LDH/GQDs electrocatalysts. (d-f) The calculated OER free-energy diagram on the Ni metal sites for Ni-based LDH@GQDs and Ni-based LDH model at different potentials. A lower free-energy value shows higher intrinsic OER activity.

Schematic illustration showing the fabrication (a) and synthesis principle (b) of the Ni-based LDH/GQDs nanosheet arrays. SEM images of sheet-like (c) NiFe LDH@GQD, (d) NiCo LDH@GQD and (e) NiMn LDH@GQD nanoarrays (the insets are magnifying images). The EDS mapping images of (f) NiFe LDH@GQD, (g) NiCo LDH@GQD and (h) NiMn LDH@GQD, respectively. (i, j) TEM and HRTEM images of the NiFe LDH@GQD, (k, l) HRTEM images of the NiMn LDH@GQD and NiCo LDH@GQD, respectively.

XPS spectra of the Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts: (a) Ni 2p and (d) Fe 2p of NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH@GQD (the upper one); (b) Ni 2p and (e) Co 2p of NiCo LDH and NiCo LDH@GQD (the upper one); (c) Ni 2p and (f) Mn 2p of NiMn LDH and NiMn LDH@GQD (the upper one).

Electrochemical performance of different catalysts. (a) Cyclic voltammogram curves of NiM LDH@GQDs (M = Fe, Co and Mn) nanosheet arrays. (b) Polarization curves of NiM LDH (M = Fe, Co and Mn) and NiM LDH@GQDs (M = Fe, Co and Mn) nanosheet arrays. (c) Enlargement of the Ni2+/Ni3+ redox range in LSV curves. (d) Comparative overpotential (10 mA cm−2) and (e) Tafel plots of the different catalysts. (f) Stability tests at the potentials of 10 mA cm−2 for NiM LDH/GQD (M = Fe, Co and Mn) nanosheet arrays. (g) Reduced LSV overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 versus reduced DFT overpotential of Ni-based LDHs/GQDs, demonstrating the experimental activity–theoretical activity relationship. (h) Reduced LSV overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 versus shift degree of Ni 2p3/2 binding energy center for Ni-based LDHs/GQDs, demonstrating the Ni 2p3/2 binding energy–activity relationship. (i) Charge density difference of NiFe/G when O‒CH bonds with Ni/Fe (iso-surface levels are 0.0036 eÅ−3, cyan and yellow represent electron depletion and accumulation respectively).

Electrochemical performance of the rechargeable Zn-air batteries: (a) Schematic representation of the Zn-air battery; (b) Polarization and power density curves of the primary Zn-air batteries of NiFe LDH/GQD as electrocatalyst of air-cathode; (c) Galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling obtained at 10 mA cm−2 for the NiFe LDH/GQD based Zn-air battery; (d) The magnified cycle images (from the 60th cycle to 70th cycle).

head.ce:title: Electrostatic adsorbing graphene quantum dot into nickel–based layered double hydroxides: Electron absorption/donor effects enhanced oxygen electrocatalytic activity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Aerospace Engineering, Chongqing University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring electrocatalysts with optimal electronic structure and understanding the relationship between electronic structure and activity are highly required for designing advanced OER catalysts. Recently, introducing nanocarbon materials into LDHs has been demonstrated as an effective way to improve the OER activity; however, deeply understanding the regulating effect of nanocarbon on the electronic structure of LDHs is still a critical challenge. Herein, it is reported that the regulating of the surface electronic structure of nickel-based layered double hydroxides (NiM-LDHs (M = Fe, Co, Mn)) by adsorbing graphene quantum dot (GQDs). The Ni-based LDHs/GQDs delivers enhanced OER activity, and particularly NiFe LDH/GQDs achieves an ultralow overpotential of 189 mV (10 mA cm−2) and superior performance in rechargeable Zn–air battery tests. Combining with theoretical calculations and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, we ascribe the excellent OER activity of Ni-based LDHs/GQDs to the strong interaction between NiFe LDH and GQDs, which changes the charge distribution around metal ions and triggers the charge accumulation of the active Ni species. The above results demonstrate the significance of controlling the surface electronic structure for designing advanced 3d metal electrocatalysts.

We found that graphene quantum dots can well regulate the electronic structures of the Ni-based layered double hydroxides (LDHs). The improved OER activity is attributed to the strong electronic coupling effect between GQDs and LDHs.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) has been regarded as a significant half-reaction in rechargeable metal-air batteries, water splitting and fuel cells [1–8]. However, current OER electrocatalysts with low electrocatalytic activities lead to the high charging overpotential, which has severely limited the application value of rechargeable metal-air batteries [9,10]. So far, tuning the surface electronic structures of the electrocatalysts has been recognized as the one of the most important strategies to optimize their electrocatalytic activities [11–19]. Several recent studies about surface electronic structures modulation have remarkably improved the intrinsic activities of the OER electrocatalysts, such as, (1) metal cations doping: introducing Fe2+ to adjust the metal ions in layered double hydroxide and Co2+/Co3+ ions doped in NiFe-layered double hydroxide [11,12]; (2) in-situ hybridizations: single-atom Au grew on NiFe LDH and MoS2/Fe-NiCo2O4 heterojunctions [13,16]. The primary reason is that the metal cations or novel composition can effectively optimize electronic structure of the active centers through the new formed covalent bonds, further optimize the adsorption energy of the active centers with the intermediates. However, deeper insight on the relationship between surface electronic structures and electrocatalytic activities is still highly challenging, but meaningful and valuable.

For now, among the high-profile 3d metal-hydr(oxy)oxide electrocatalysts, Ni-based compound have been demonstrated as a benchmark electrocatalysts with superior OER activities, because Ni species with its optimal interaction strength with adsorbed OH can well satisfy the Sabatier principle for electrocatalyst design [20]. In particular, thanks to the opening layered structure providing rich redox activity sites, the Ni-based layered double hydroxide (LDH) materials such as NiFe-LDHs generally possesses remarkable OER performance [21–30], which stimulates the endless exploration and optimization of OER properties on LDH-based materials. Recent works have demonstrated that the combining of nanocarbons and LDHs can effectively enhance the OER activity by interfacial charge transferring [31–33]. For instance, Yin et al. found that the introduction of nanocarbon can adjust the electronic properties of Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions by the interfacial electronic coupling of NiFe LDH and C, thus endowing the NiFe LDHs a remarkably improved OER activity with good durability [33]. However, the regulating effect of the nanocarbon on the electronic structure of LDHs is still unclear and further efforts are highly demanded. Very recently, as an emerging class of nanocarbons, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified electrocatalysts have been found to exhibit higher oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activities [34,35], by increasing the electrochemical active surface areas as well as introducing quantum effects to regulate the electronic structures. However, to date, there has been little research on whether GQDs contributes to improving the OER electrocatalytic activity of current electrocatalysts, especially LDHs.

Here, we initiated the combining of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the Ni-based LDHs system by employing electrostatic adsorption effect and successfully prepared nanoarray structured Ni-based LDHs /GQDs hybrids, the OER activity of which was assessed and the relationships between surface electronic structure and OER activity were deeply investigated. According to XPS and LSV, we find that the valence state of the metal ions in Ni-based LDHs/GQDs electrocatalyst is closely related to the OER performance, and its desirable nanoarray structure further lower the OER overpotential to 189 mV (10 mA cm−2), which is comparable to one of the excellent Ni-based OER catalysts for now. Benefiting from the prominent OER performance, the charge potential of Zn-air battery with NiFe LDH/GQDs is obviously lower than that of Zn-air battery with NiFe LDH. Combining with DFT calculation results, we confirm that the electrons transferring from LDH to GQDs, which push up the valence state of metal ions and optimize the adsorption energy of LDHs for intermediates, can significantly improve OER intrinsic activity of LDHs. In addition, some positive correlation between electronic structure and OER activity is also revealed, that is Ni-based LDHs/GQDs with higher OER activity have larger shift degree in Ni 2p3/2 binding energy, higher Ni oxidation state and more charge increment around Ni. In short, it is clearly demonstrated that the significant role of surface electronic structure on optimizing the properties of Ni-based LDHs OER electrocatalyst, and the refined design of surface electronic structure have important implications for the design and synthesis of other catalysts.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations are firstly executed to explore whether GQDs adsorbed onto the surface of LDHs has beneficial and universal effect on the OER electrocatalytic activities in theory. The brucite-like layers in LDHs contain the divalent/trivalent metal cations with definite proportion, where cations coordinated octahedral by hydroxyl groups [36]. Take the example of NiFe LDH, a Ni site is surrounded by three neighboring Ni atoms and Fe atoms (Ni:Fe = 2:1), respectively, thus Ni-O‒Ni or Ni‒O‒Fe chemical bonds are presented in the NiFe LDH. After introducing GQDs into the system, due to the electrostatic adsorption effect between LDHs and GQDs, a strong electronic interaction between them is probably formed. A series of first-principles calculations are conducted by using DFT methods, and the simulation model for NiFe LDH/GQDs and pure NiFe LDH is shown as Fig. 1a and b (NiCo LDH/GQDs and NiMn LDH/GQDs are presented in Fig. S1). The model consists of a LDH layer and a single C atom with O/H on the LDH’s surface.

Fig. 1 c shows the optimized pathways of the OER at Ni metal sites of NiFe LDH/GQDs catalyst. It is known that the OER process in alkaline electrolytes experiences four elementary steps: M*, M‒OH, M‒O, and M‒OOH (see Supplementary Information for details). The OER electrocatalytic activity is evaluated by the adsorption intensity of the catalyst surface for intermediates (OH*, O*, OOH*) and the free energy difference during the OER steps. According to the free energy diagram of NiFe LDH/GQDs and NiFe LDH (Fig. 1d), the formation of O* to OOH* course is the potential determining step for the OER process, and the calculated overpotential obviously decreased from 0.95 V for NiFe LDH to 0.451 V for NiFe LDH/GQDs, suggesting a promising improvement on the OER electrocatalytic activity of NiFe LDH. Similar results are demonstrated in Fig. 1e and f, where the overpotentials of NiCo LDH/GQDs and NiMn LDH/GQDs are observably lower than those of the corresponding NiCo LDH and NiMn LDH, respectively. Hence，it is concluded that the introducing of GQDs can improve OER activity of Ni-based LDHs. Furthermore, the reduction degree of OER overpotentials for LDHs after coupling with GQDs is NiCo LDH/GQDs (0.589 V) > NiFe LDH/GQDs (0.499 V) > NiMn LDH/GQDs (0.192 V). Noteworthy, the potential determining step is changed from “O* to OH*” for pure NiCo LDH to “O* to OOH*” for NiCo LDH/GODs after absorbing GQDs, along with sharp decrease in the calculated OER overpotential. These results imply that the interfacial sites at the LDHs-GQDs interfaces play a crucial role in improving OER activity, especially for NiCo LDH. Besides, previous reports have already shown that the interlayer CO3 2- anions and H2O in LDHs can maintain the charge balance of the LDHs with new introduced atoms, which is beneficial to stabilize the structure of the LDHs/GQDs [16].

Intrigued by the calculated results, we explored to design a general and facile approach to couple GQDs with Ni-based LDHs to further verify the positive role of GQDs experimentally. Here, all Ni-based LDH@GQD electrocatalysts were in-situ grown onto Ni foams to improve their OER electrocatalytic activities because of the open framework structure and good electronic conductivity. Before synthesizing the Ni-based LDH@GQD arrays, we firstly measured their surface charges of Ni-based LDH colloids prepared by the same chemical method and GOQDs. The as-collected Ni-based LDH colloids have positively charged surfaces (zeta potentials are 11.4, 24.8 and 20.6 mV for NiFe LDH, NiCo LDH and NiMn LDH, respectively), owing to that the brucite-like layers in LDHs are positively charged [34]. For GOQDs, with many surface oxygen-containing functional groups [38], it is negatively charged with a zeta potential of − 31.5 mV. Thus, inspired by the typical principle of colloid flocculation in colloid chemistry, an electrostatic attractive method is used to synthesize the nanoarray structured Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts in this study. Fig. 2a and b schematically illustrates the experimental procedure and basic principle. The preparation of sheet-like Ni-based LDH nanoarrays involves the precipitation of metal nitrates on Ni foam that was triggered by urea hydrolysis, during which the generated CO3 2− species serve as the intercalated anions [39]. Subsequently, the nanoarray structured Ni-based LDH@GQD hybrids are obtained by the spontaneously electrostatic absorption and hydrothermal reduction. As judged by color changes of Ni-based LDH@nickel foam, it can indicate the existing of GQDs, as depicted in Fig. S2. The XRD patterns in Fig. S3 display the crystalline structures and phase purities of the as-synthesized Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts on Ni foam. Except for the three strongest peaks corresponding to Ni, the (003) and (006) crystal faces of corresponding to thickness of LDH layer, (012) and (015) crystal faces of determining the stacking pattern of the layers and the number of layers stacked, (110) crystal face corresponded to the distance between two positive ion in the host layer are observed for all samples [37]. These crystal faces are characteristic peaks of a typical LDH phase. The characteristic (002) peaks（PDF#41-1487）for carbon at ~26° are also observed for all three samples [40], suggesting the successful compositing of GQDs with LDH. The interlayer spacing of LDH is an important structural index for OER activity, therefore, XRD patterns of the powder-like NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH/G are carefully examined (Fig. S4), which shows only a slight peak shift of the (003) and (006) characteristic peaks. The calculated interlayer spacing of NiFe LDHs increases by ~0.1 Å after hybriding with GODs, indicating the introduction of GQDs has no obviously influence on the interlayer spacing of NiFe LDH. Consistent with the XRD results, Raman spectrum of the NiFe LDH/GQDs displays obvious peaks of G band (~1580 cm−1) and D band (~1340 cm−1) of graphene and LDH phase (~520 and 690 cm−1) [33,39,41,42], respectively (Fig. S5). Comparing with GOQDs (ID/IG=0.96), an increased ID/IG ratio of 1.06 for the NiFe LDH/GQDs is detected, indicating a decrease in the size of the in-plane sp2 domains and the partial removal of the oxygen functional groups after hydrothermal reduction [43].

The morphology and microstructure was further disclosed by SEM, as shown in Fig. 2c-e. Obviously, the ordered arrangement nanosheets growing vertically on Ni foam can be observed, thus a uniform and porous LDH layers are obtained. However, the lateral sizes, thickness and growth orientations are different for different Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts. In addition, the GQDs are invisible in these SEM images owing to the ultra-small size (Fig. S6). The EDS mapping results of the Ni-based LDH/GQD nanoarrays in Fig. 2f-h show a uniform element distribution include Ni, Fe, Co, Mn, C and O, which demonstrates the formation of the Ni-based LDHs with two types of metal ions and the existence of GQDs. Fig. 2i-l and S7 show the TEM and HRTEM images of Ni-based LDH/GQDs scraping off Ni foam. The GQDs with an average diameter of 5–10 nm are uniformly and densely decorated onto the LDH nanosheets, especially on the edge of LDH. The lattice spacing of GQDs is 0.21 nm, which corresponding to the (1100) lattice plane of graphene [44]. Two interplanar distance of 0.2 nm, 0.23 nm and 0.24 nm are measured for a single crystal particle, corresponding to the (018) and (015) plane of the LDH, respectively. Notably, there are no obvious transition structure characteristics between LDH and GQDs. This is consistent with our analysis of the surface charge characteristics of LDHs and GOQDs.

To uncover the influence of the interfacial electronic structure on electrocatalytic activities, the bonding states of Ni-based LDHs and Ni-based LDH/GQDs are discussed by XPS ( Fig. 3, S8 and S9). The full XPS spectra (Fig. S8) show the peaks located at ~712 eV (NiFe LDHs and NiFe LDH/GQD), ~780 eV (NiCo LDHs and NiCo LDH/GQD) and ~642 eV (NiMn LDHs and NiMn LDH/GQD), corresponding to the 2p levels of Fe2+, Co2+/3+ and Mn2+/3+/4+, respectively. Comparing with Ni-based LDHs, the content of carbon atoms increases from 20–30 at% to above 50 at% for Ni-based LDH/GQDs as delivered in Table S1, demonstrating again that the GQDs adsorbed onto Ni-based LDHs successfully. On the contrary, the oxygen contents are decreased from 40–50 at% for GOQDs to 15–32 at% for Ni-based LDH/GQDs, further indicating the reduction of GOQDs during hydrothermal process. It is found in Fig. 3a and d that the high-resolution Ni 2p3/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peaks of NiFe LDHs are shifted to higher values after coupled with GQDs, resulting in the emergence of new Ni3+ ions. What’s more, the content of Ni3+ ions is close to that of Ni2+ ions (Ni3+/Ni2+ ratio is 0.985:1). These results show an interfacial electronic coupling in the electrocatalyst, then resulting in the occurrence of electron transfer between NiFe LDH and GQDs. Such phenomenon can be called as electron absorption/donor effects. Similarly, all 2p3/2 peak centers of the metal ions shift with various degrees for NiCo LDH@GQD and NiMn LDH@GQD samples, leading to a higher valence state of all Ni species (Fig. 3b, c, e and f). However, for NiCo LDH@GQD, the interaction of Ni ions and GQDs should be primary contribution for charge transfer, while little change of Co3+/Co2+ ratio was observed. Furthermore, the appearance of Ni3+ oxidation state (856.1 eV) and the obvious increasing of Mn4+/Mn3+ atomic ratio together with the decrease of Mn2+, implying that the GQDs have strong effect on Ni and Mn ions, respectively. Previous researches already demonstrate that Ni, Fe, Mn and Co ions with higher valence state, such as Ni3+/4+, Fe4+, Mn3+ and Co3+, are favorable for enhancing the OER electrocatalytic activities in the Ni-based hydroxides [12,14,45,46], owing to the minishing of conduction band of electrocatalysts and increasing of electrophilic centers during OER course [14,45–47]. Therefore, pre-oxidizing of metal ions from low valence state to high valence state by the introduction of GQDs is a reasonable concept for OER electrocatalysts. The following C 1s spectra of all samples show that the probable existence of C‒OH bond at ~286 eV as depicted in Fig. S9, indicating the strong electronic interaction between LDHs and GQDs, and the left shifts of C˭O (on behalf of CO3 2−) binding energy mean that the increasing charges around metal ions requires the compensation of additional anions such as OH- and CO3 2− to maintain the charge balance of LDHs [48]. The electronic coupling effect should be a key factor for optimizing the OER adsorption energy of LDHs electrocatalysts, which will facilitate the charge transport and favor the OER process. Similar types are also found in the NiFe-LDH/C and NiFe LDH/CNTs composites [33,49], where the interaction of LDHs and C is formed via the carboxyl group and metal elements. Except the experimental results, MD simulations using the reactive force field (ReaxFF) potential suggests the strong interaction between the GQD and metal oxide [44,50].

The OER activities of Ni-based LDHs and Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts are investigated. The CV curves of these Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts in Fig. 4a reveal the Ni2+/Ni3+ oxidation peaks centered at 1.37–1.43 V versus RHE [39]. The positive (oxidation) current at potentials greater than 1.5 V vs. RHE is ascribed to OER process. In the IR-compensated OER polarization curves (Fig. 4b), the optimal NiFe LDH/GQD electrocatalyst deliver an overpotentials of 189 mV at 10 mA cm−2, which is 23 mV lower than that of the pure NiFe LDH, manifesting an enhanced OER electrocatalytic activity. Our overpotential value is comparable with those of current best Ni-based oxide/hydroxides OER electrocatalysts (Table S2). And the value (189 mV) is much superior to those of the state-of-the-art Ir/C electrocatalysts [51]. Likewise, the OER activities of NiCo LDH/GQD and NiMn LDH/GQD are also superior to those of the NiCo LDH and NiMn LDH (Fig. 4d), with overpotential reduction of 33 mV (NiCo LDH/GQD) and 15 mV (NiMn LDH/GQD), respectively. Besides, the magnified LSV curves also show that the new chemical environments brought by the absorbed GQDs can alter the recyclable Ni2+/Ni3+ oxidation peaks at 1.3–1.4 V (Fig. 4c). The smaller Tafel slopes, charge transfer resistances and increased ECSA further demonstrate the accelerated OER kinetics and improved OER performance of the Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts, as displayed in Fig. 4e and S10-S12. Since their morphology, size, crystal structure and ECSA have no obvious difference, we infer that the modulated electronic structure is more inportant reason for the enhanced OER activity of Ni-based LDH/GQD. Moreover, these results also reveal a strong positive correlation between metal ions with higher valence state and the OER electrocatalytic activity. The improving degree of OER activities and the shift distances of binding energy of Ni, Fe, Co and Mn 2p peaks reflect the positive correlation. The galvanostatic tests of 15 h show that the relative stable OER currents for the Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts, suggesting good long term stabilities (Fig. 4f). The chemical states after the OER durability test are also examined, by taking NiFe/GQD as an example. The 2p3/2 peak centers of Ni and Fe shift to positions with lower binding energy (Ni 2p3/2: ~0.7 eV; Fe 2p3/2: ~1.2 eV), as depicted in Fig. S13, indicating the relatively lower Ni and Fe oxidation state after OER durability test. This result is similar to that of the previous reported NiFe/C electrocatalysts [30]. According to the CV curves in Fig. 4a, the electrocatalyst will firstly undergo an oxidation process with NiFeOOH as the intermediates [14], and then OER will take place. The as-formed higher oxidation states of Ni and Fe atoms are unstable, which will further be reduced to their corresponding balanced states after exposing the surfaces of NiFe LDH/GQDs electrocatalysts to moisture in ambient conditions. This is the reason for the XPS peak shifts of Ni and Fe 2p3/2 after OER test.

To confirm the consistency of the theoretical calculation and the experiment results, the experiment overpotential in LSV and the theoretical overpotential in DFT are synchronously shown in Fig. 4g and h. After the introducing of GQDs, the activities of the Ni-based LDHs/GQDs are obviously enhanced both from the theoretical calculation and the experiment results. What's more, a strong positive correlation between the theoretical calculation and the experiment can be clearly demonstrated: the trend in reduced values of theoretical overpotential consistent well with that of the experiment overpotential, for example, NiCo LDH/GQDs with the largest reduction degree (0.589 eV) by DFT calculation have the largest reduced values (33 mV) in LSV (Fig. 4g). In addition, we find that, comparing with NiMn LDHs/GQDs, NiFe LDHs/GQDs and NiCo LDH/GQDs with higher activity have larger shift degree in Ni 2p3/2 binding energy (Fig. 4h). These results confirm that the Ni oxidation states and OER activity have some positive correlation. Although the shift degree of Ni 2p3/2 binding energy is tiny, the large change of valence state for Mn ions should be the reason for the improvement in activity of NiMn LDH/GQDs. The above results are consistent with our previous work [19]: the higher valence state of metal ions in LDHs is favorable for OER activity.

In order to further confirm the enhanced mechanism of OER activity by the electron absorption/donor effects, we theoretically investigate the differences of surface charge of Ni-based LDH/GQDs and Ni-based LDH electrocatalysts (Fig. 4i and S14). Electron depletion around Ni and M atoms after the combining of LDHs and GQDs, which leading to observably increasing of the charge around Ni and M atoms within Ni-based LDH/GQDs, especially for Ni atoms (Table S3). Move over, there are highly consistency among the charge increment around Ni/M(Fe, Co, Mn), changing of OER activity and shift degree in Ni 2p3/2 binding energy, which further demonstrate the positive correlation between electronic structure and OER activity. More important, the differential charge confirms that the electrons transfer from LDHs to GQDs, which would elevate the valence state of metal centers, thus strengthening the adsorption capacity of OH- (to maintain charge balance in LDHs) and optimizing the adsorption energies of intermediates, resulting in low overpotential in the OER step.

Previous work already verified that the ORR activities of LDH-based electrocatalysts are negligible [21,31,51]. However, for practical applications such as rechargeable Zn-air battery, the ORR performance of electrocatalysts is also an important parameter. Fig. S15 recorded the ORR polarization curves of the Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts and Ni-based LDHs. The largest current densities (0.29 mA cm−2) of the pure NiFe LDH are less than one third of that of the NiFe LDH with GQD (0.95 mA cm−2 at −0.8 V vs. RHE). For NiCo LDH/GQD and NiMn LDH/GQD, the half-wave potentials are much more positive than those of the corresponding NiCo LDH and NiMn LDH, respectively. Clearly, coupling with GQDs is beneficial to the ORR performance for Ni-based LDHs. Summarily, these results demonstrate that the integration of GQDs and LDHs synchronously improve the OER and ORR activities of LDHs, especially for NiFe LDH. Hence, the surface electronic structure modification of GQDs is an effective strategy for designing of LDH-based bifunctional electrocatalysts.

To evaluate the practical application of the Ni-based LDH/GQD electrocatalysts in rechargeable Zn-air battery, a home-made liquid-phase cell was constructed with NiFe LDH/GQD as the air-cathode electrocatalysts due to the best OER performance and improved ORR electrocatalytic activity, as shown in Fig. 5a. The assembled Zn-air battery delivers moderate specific capacity of 677.4 mAh g−1 and peak power density of 140.4 mW cm−2 but high energy density of 802.8 Wh kg−1 (Fig. 5b). The cyclic performance is depicted in Fig. 5c and d. Attractively, the constructed Zn-air battery demonstrates extremely stable performance over 120 h of continuous testing and more than 500 cycles. The voltage gap of charge and discharge processes is only 0.73–0.75 V, initial round-trip efficiency reaches 61.1%, showing its outstanding reversibility. Thanks to the improved OER and ORR performance, the Zn–air battery with NiFe LDH/GQD electrocatalysts shows obviously smaller voltage difference than that with NiFe LDH electrocatalysts during battery operation (Fig. 5d). The charge/discharge overpotential reduced by only ~37 mV (30 mV of OER and 7 mV of ORR). It is meaning that the Zn–air battery with NiFe LDH/GQD has lower overpotential and higher round-trip efficiency.

In summary, by means of DFT, XPS and electrochemical analysis, we demonstrate that modulating the surface electronic structure within the Ni-based LDH structure via electrostatic adsorbing grapheme quantum dot is an effective strategy to enhance the OER activity. The electron absorption/donor effects between Ni-based LDHs and GQDs plays a key role on the significantly improved OER intrinsic activity of Ni-based LDHs. The effect causes the electrons transfer from Ni-based LDHs to GQDs, which adjusts the charge distribution and thus further enhancing the OER activity. It is found that some positive correlation between Ni oxidation state and OER activity, which can offer some guide for designing top electrocatalysts with favorable surface electronic environment. The rechargeable Zn–air battery using NiFe-LDH/GQDs shows a high round-trip efficiency (> 60%) with a small voltage gap of 0.75 V and outstanding durability. This work will open a new avenue to design advanced LDHs bifunctional catalysts as a replacement of precious metals for Zn–air batteries.

X. Guo and C. Xu conceived the idea and designed the experiments. C. Xu and F. Pan provided financial support through grant application. X. Guo and X. Hu performed the experiments. X. Zheng and L. Li conducted the theoretical calculations. Q. Zhao, P. Yu, C. Jing, and Y. Zhang helped with data analysis. All authors participated in discussing and writing the manuscript, read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603019, 201503025), Key Program for International Science and Technology Cooperation Projects of Ministry of Science and Technology of China (No. 2016YFE0125900) and program for the Hundred Talents Program of Chongqing University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2021.105932.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaolong Guo is now a lecturer of Physics and Electronic Engineering at Chongqing normal University. He completed his B.S. degree in Materials Science and Engineering from Chongqing University of Technology in 2011. He received his master’s degree in Materials Science and Engineering from Chongqing University in 2016, and Ph.D. degree in the College of Aerospace Engineering Chongqing University with Prof. Chaohe Xu in 2019. His current research focuses on the rational design and fabrication of nanostructured materials as electrocatalysts for Zn-air batteries.

Xingqun Zheng received her B.S. degree in 2015 from China West Normal University (CWNU). Now, she is studying towards a Ph.D. degree in the school of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in Chongqing University (CQU), China. Her current research interests are in the computational studies of electrochemical and electrocatalytic processes for water splitting and fuel cell.

Xiaolin Hu received her B.S. degree in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering from Hainan Normal University in 2016. Following this, she entered in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Chongqing University as a Master student. She is now pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Chaohe Xu in the College of Aerospace Engineering, Chongqing University. Her current research focuses on the development of functional nanomaterials and their applications in secondary batteries and electrocatalysis.

Qiannan Zhao received her B.S. degree in School of Materials Science and Engineering from Wuhan University of Technology, P.R. China. She is now pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Chaohe Xu in the College of Aerospace Engineering, Chongqing University, P.R. China. Her research is mainly focused on high performance Metal-Sulfur batteries such as Li-S batteries and Mg-S batteries.

Li Li obtained her MS and PhD in 2004 and 2010 from Chongqing University; and now she is a Professor in the same University. Her main interests are in the fundamental studies of electrochemical and electrocatalytic processes through the theoretical investigations. She uses density functional theory to explore the relationship between the catalytic activity and the electronic structure of the catalysts, and understand the mechanism of enhanced catalytic activity and stability of different kinds of materials.

Peng Yu received his B.S. degree in 2002 in the college of physics Chongqing University and Ph.D. in 2008 in institute of physics from Chinese Academy of sciences. He also worked successively as a research associate and postdoctoral fellow in the Hong Kong Polytechnic University. He is currently a Professor in College of Physics and Electronic Engineering at Chongqing Normal University. His current interests focus on metal function materials, nanocomposites for electrocatalysis.

Chuan Jing received his Ph.D. degree in 2020 in the College of Materials Science and Engineering of Chongqing University. He is currently an Assistant Professor in Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications. His current interests include the synthesis and characterization of layered double hydroxides with various chemical modification methods in supercapacitor.

Yuxin Zhang received his B.Eng. and M. Eng. in Chemical Engineering from Tianjin University in 2000 and 2003, respectively. He received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering from the National University of Singapore (NUS) in 2008, and continued to work as a research fellow in Prof. Hua Chun Zeng’s group at NUS until 2009. Now Dr Zhang is a full professor in the College of Materials Science and Engineering in Chongqing University. His research interest is self-assembled nanostructures for energy storage materials.

Guangsheng Huang is a Professor at the College of Materials Science and Engineering in Chongqing University. He obtained his BS and MS degrees from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 1997 and 2000 respectively, and obtained his PhD from Chongqing University in 2003. From 2006–2007, he was a visit scholar at Helsinki University of Technology in Finland; 2017–2018, he was a visit scholar at North Carolina State University in the United States. Now his research interests mainly include rechargeable batteries, magnesium-air batteries.

Bin Jiang is a Changjiang Distinguished Professor at the College of Materials Science and Engineering in Chongqing University. Prof. Jiang is the Deputy Director of National Engineering and Technology Research Center for Magnesium Alloy Material of China. He received BS degree in Metallurgical and Material Engineering from Chongqing University in 1997, and master's and doctorate degree in Materials Science and Engineering from Chongqing University in 2000 and 2003. He was a visiting scholar at the University of Queensland in 2008. His current researches include magnesium alloy sheet and strip processing, thin strip casting and rolling and nano surface engineering.

Chaohe Xu is a tenured associate professor at the College of Aerospace Engineering in Chongqing University. He received BS degree in Materials Science and Engineering from Tianjin University in 2007, and PhD degree in Materials Physics and Chemistry from Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2012. In 2013–2015, he worked as a postdoctoral research fellow at Department of Chemical and Molecular Biological Engineering, National University of Singapore. Then, he moved to Chongqing University as an assistant professor in 2015–2020. His current research interests include rechargeable lithium/sodium batteries, metal-air batteries, solid state electrolytes, and supercapacitors.

Fusheng Pan is an Academician of Chinese Academy of Engineering, professor in Chongqing University. He received his Doctors degree in Materials Science and Engineering at Northwestern Polytechnical University in 1995. Prof. Pan also studied and/or worked in Oxford University, Stuttgart University, Chiba University, University of California, Queen Mary, and University of Queensland. He is a foreign member of Academy of Mining Sciences in Russia, member of Asian Pacific Academy of Materials. He serves as director of National Engineering Research Center for Magnesium Alloys. He specializes in the research and development of magnesium alloys, magnesium matrix composites and magnesium-based functional materials.
